advantages: Provide a unified language (PMF, PDF, CDF) for reasoning about uncertainty in both discrete labels and continuous measurements.
Enable precise probability statements—P(X ≤ x), P(a ≤ X ≤ b)—that underpin loss functions, metrics, and risk thresholds in ML.
Separate discrete and continuous cases cleanly, guiding correct use of sums vs integrals and preventing category/probability confusions.
The CDF works for any random variable and connects directly to sampling (inverse CDF), percentile SLAs, and calibration analysis.
disadvantages: PDF values are densities, not probabilities—learners often misread f(x) as P(X = x), leading to incorrect conclusions for continuous variables.
High-dimensional random variables (images, token sequences) rarely have tractable closed-form PDFs; exact PMF/PDF reasoning breaks down without approximation or generative model assumptions.
Real data is often mixed (censored, rounded, or zero-inflated), so strict discrete/continuous labels can be oversimplified without careful modeling.
Empirical distributions from finite samples only approximate true PMFs/PDFs; small data can produce misleading histograms or density estimates.
